CVE Description:
Shopware is an open source e-commerce software platform. Prior to 6.6.10.3 or 6.5.8.17, the default settings for double-opt-in allow for mass unsolicited newsletter sign-ups without confirmation. Default settings are Newsletter: Double Opt-in set to active, Newsletter: Double opt-in for registered customers set to disabled, and Log-in & sign-up: Double opt-in on sign-up set to disabled. With these settings, anyone can register an account on the shop using any e-mail-address and then check the check-box in the account page to sign up for the newsletter. The recipient will receive two mails confirming registering and signing up for the newsletter, no confirmation link needed to be clicked for either. In the backend the recipient is set to “instantly active”. This vulnerability is fixed in 6.6.10.3 or 6.5.8.17.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is the system's failure to verify the user's control over an email address before performing the sensitive action of subscribing it to a newsletter. Due to the default configuration, the double-opt-in confirmation step, which serves as the authentication mechanism for this action, is completely bypassed for users who have registered an account (even if that account's email was never verified). The system performs a critical function (newsletter subscription) without any authentication of the true principal (the email owner).

This directly maps to the weakness of a critical function lacking an authentication check.

Answer: CWE-306

CVE Description:
The V8 release used in Node.js v24.0.0 has changed how string hashes are computed using rapidhash. This implementation re-introduces the HashDoS vulnerability as an attacker who can control the strings to be hashed can generate many hash collisions - an attacker can generate collisions even without knowing the hash-seed. * This vulnerability affects Node.js v24.x users.
response:
The vulnerability described is a "HashDoS" attack, which is a specific type of algorithmic complexity attack. An attacker can provide specially crafted input (strings) that all result in the same hash value. When these strings are inserted into a hash table, they all land in the same bucket, degrading the performance of hash table operations from an average of O(1) to a worst-case of O(n). This leads to excessive CPU consumption and a denial of service. This scenario is precisely described by CWE-407, which covers vulnerabilities where an attacker can force an algorithm into its worst-case performance scenario.

Answer: CWE-407

A vulnerability was found in Keycloak. Admin users may have to access sensitive server environment variables and system properties through user-configurable URLs. When configuring backchannel logout URLs or admin URLs, admin users can include placeholders like ${env.VARNAME} or ${PROPNAME}. The server replaces these placeholders with the actual values of environment variables or system properties during URL processing.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is that the application processes user-supplied input (a URL) that contains special syntax (`${...}`) characteristic of an Expression Language (EL). The server fails to properly neutralize or sanitize these special elements, causing it to interpret and evaluate the expression. This evaluation leads to the replacement of the placeholder with sensitive server-side information, such as environment variables and system properties, which are then exposed. This is a classic example of Expression Language Injection.

CVE Description:
An access control vulnerability was discovered in Grafana OSS where an Organization administrator could permanently delete the Server administrator account. This vulnerability exists in the DELETE /api/org/users/ endpoint. The vulnerability can be exploited when: 1. An Organization administrator exists 2. The Server administrator is either: - Not part of any organization, or - Part of the same organization as the Organization administrator Impact: - Organization administrators can permanently delete Server administrator accounts - If the only Server administrator is deleted, the Grafana instance becomes unmanageable - No super-user permissions remain in the system - Affects all users, organizations, and teams managed in the instance The vulnerability is particularly serious as it can lead to a complete loss of administrative control over the Grafana instance.
response:

The system correctly identifies the user as an "Organization administrator" and permits them to access the user deletion endpoint. However, the authorization check is incorrect because it fails to properly validate that the target of the deletion action (the "Server administrator") is not a user with higher privileges than the actor. A lower-privileged user is incorrectly authorized to perform a destructive action on a higher-privileged user, which is a classic case of incorrect authorization.

response:

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: i3c: dw: Fix use-after-free in dw_i3c_master driver due to race condition In dw_i3c_common_probe, &master->hj_work is bound with dw_i3c_hj_work. And dw_i3c_master_irq_handler can call dw_i3c_master_irq_handle_ibis function to start the work. If we remove the module which will call dw_i3c_common_remove to make cleanup, it will free master->base through i3c_master_unregister while the work mentioned above will be used. The sequence of operations that may lead to a UAF bug is as follows: CPU0 CPU1 | dw_i3c_hj_work dw_i3c_common_remove | i3c_master_unregister(&master->base) | device_unregister(&master->dev) | device_release | //free master->base | | i3c_master_do_daa(&master->base) | //use master->base Fix it by ensuring that the work is canceled before proceeding with the cleanup in dw_i3c_common_remove.
response:
The CVE description explicitly states the vulnerability is a "use-after-free ... due to race condition". The core issue is the lack of synchronization between two concurrent operations: the module removal process which frees a resource (`master->base`), and a scheduled work item (`dw_i3c_hj_work`) which uses that same resource. The vulnerability occurs because the cleanup/free operation can happen before the work item has finished its execution, leading to the work item accessing deallocated memory. This is a classic example of a race condition where the program's correctness depends on the unpredictable timing of concurrent threads. While the result is a Use-After-Free (CWE-416), the fundamental flaw that enables it is the improper synchronization.

CVE Description:
A vulnerability has been identified within Rancher where a cluster or node driver can be used to escape the chroot jail and gain root access to the Rancher container itself. In production environments, further privilege escalation is possible based on living off the land within the Rancher container itself. For the test and development environments, based on a –privileged Docker container, it is possible to escape the Docker container and gain execution access on the host system. This issue affects rancher: from 2.7.0 before 2.7.16, from 2.8.0 before 2.8.9, from 2.9.0 before 2.9.3.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is the failure to properly confine a process within its designated directory, allowing it to access resources outside of that boundary. The description explicitly states that a "cluster or node driver can be used to escape the chroot jail". A chroot jail's primary purpose is to restrict a process's root directory. Escaping this jail almost always involves manipulating a file path to traverse up the directory tree (e.g., using `../`) and break out of the intended confinement. This is the classic definition of a Path Traversal vulnerability.

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: fs/ntfs3: provide block_invalidate_folio to fix memory leak The ntfs3 filesystem lacks the 'invalidate_folio' method and it causes memory leak. If you write to the filesystem and then unmount it, the cached written data are not freed and they are permanently leaked.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is a classic memory leak. The ntfs3 filesystem driver allocates memory to cache data that is written to it. However, it fails to implement the necessary `invalidate_folio` method, which is responsible for cleaning up and freeing this cached data when the filesystem is unmounted. As a result, the memory's effective lifetime has ended (the filesystem is no longer mounted), but it is not released, leading to a permanent leak. This directly corresponds to the definition of a missing release of memory.

CVE Description:
Strawberry GraphQL is a library for creating GraphQL APIs. Starting in 0.182.0 and prior to version 0.257.0, a type confusion vulnerability exists in Strawberry GraphQL's relay integration that affects multiple ORM integrations (Django, SQLAlchemy, Pydantic). The vulnerability occurs when multiple GraphQL types are mapped to the same underlying model while using the relay node interface. When querying for a specific type using the global node field (e.g., FruitType:some-id), the resolver may incorrectly return an instance of a different type mapped to the same model (e.g., SpecialFruitType). This can lead to information disclosure if the alternate type exposes sensitive fields and potential privilege escalation if the alternate type contains data intended for restricted access. This vulnerability is fixed in 0.257.0.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is a type confusion. The system is asked to resolve a node of a specific GraphQL type (e.g., `FruitType`), but due to a flaw in how it handles multiple types mapped to the same underlying data model, it can incorrectly process and return the data as a different, incompatible GraphQL type (e.g., `SpecialFruitType`). This misinterpretation of the object's type allows an attacker to access fields and data that should have been restricted under the originally requested type, which is the exact definition of a type confusion weakness. The CVE description explicitly labels this as a "type confusion vulnerability."

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: block: Prevent potential deadlocks in zone write plug error recovery Zone write plugging for handling writes to zones of a zoned block device always execute a zone report whenever a write BIO to a zone fails. The intent of this is to ensure that the tracking of a zone write pointer is always correct to ensure that the alignment to a zone write pointer of write BIOs can be checked on submission and that we can always correctly emulate zone append operations using regular write BIOs. However, this error recovery scheme introduces a potential deadlock if a device queue freeze is initiated while BIOs are still plugged in a zone write plug and one of these write operation fails. In such case, the disk zone write plug error recovery work is scheduled and executes a report zone. This in turn can result in a request allocation in the underlying driver to issue the report zones command to the device. But with the device queue freeze already started, this allocation will block, preventing the report zone execution and the continuation of the processing of the plugged BIOs. As plugged BIOs hold a queue usage reference, the queue freeze itself will never complete, resulting in a deadlock. Avoid this problem by completely removing from the zone write plugging code the use of report zones operations after a failed write operation, instead relying on the device user to either execute a report zones, reset the zone, finish the zone, or give up writing to the device (which is a fairly common pattern for file systems which degrade to read-only after write failures). This is not an unreasonnable requirement as all well-behaved applications, FSes and device mapper already use report zones to recover from write errors whenever possible by comparing the current position of a zone write pointer with what their assumption about the position is. The changes to remove the automatic error recovery are as follows: - Completely remove the error recovery work and its associated resources (zone write plug list head, disk error list, and disk zone_wplugs_work work struct). This also removes the functions disk_zone_wplug_set_error() and disk_zone_wplug_clear_error(). - Change the BLK_ZONE_WPLUG_ERROR zone write plug flag into BLK_ZONE_WPLUG_NEED_WP_UPDATE. This new flag is set for a zone write plug whenever a write opration targetting the zone of the zone write plug fails. This flag indicates that the zone write pointer offset is not reliable and that it must be updated when the next report zone, reset zone, finish zone or disk revalidation is executed. - Modify blk_zone_write_plug_bio_endio() to set the BLK_ZONE_WPLUG_NEED_WP_UPDATE flag for the target zone of a failed write BIO. - Modify the function disk_zone_wplug_set_wp_offset() to clear this new flag, thus implementing recovery of a correct write pointer offset with the reset (all) zone and finish zone operations. - Modify blkdev_report_zones() to always use the disk_report_zones_cb() callback so that disk_zone_wplug_sync_wp_offset() can be called for any zone marked with the BLK_ZONE_WPLUG_NEED_WP_UPDATE flag. This implements recovery of a correct write pointer offset for zone write plugs marked with BLK_ZONE_WPLUG_NEED_WP_UPDATE and within the range of the report zones operation executed by the user. - Modify blk_revalidate_seq_zone() to call disk_zone_wplug_sync_wp_offset() for all sequential write required zones when a zoned block device is revalidated, thus always resolving any inconsistency between the write pointer offset of zone write plugs and the actual write pointer position of sequential zones.
response:
The vulnerability described is a classic deadlock. The error recovery mechanism for a failed write operation attempts to allocate a new request from the device queue. However, this occurs in a context where a device queue freeze may have already been initiated. The queue freeze process, in turn, cannot complete until all outstanding queue usage references are released. The plugged BIOs, which are waiting for the error recovery to complete, hold such a reference. This creates a circular wait: the error recovery waits for the queue to unfreeze, and the queue freeze waits for the error recovery to finish. This specific type of concurrency issue, a circular wait for resources, is precisely defined by CWE-833.

Answer: CWE-833

CVE Description:
XWiki is an open-source wiki software platform. When a user without script right creates a document with an XWiki.Notifications.Code.NotificationDisplayerClass object, and later an admin edits and saves that document, the possibly malicious content of that object is output as raw HTML, allowing XSS attacks. While the notification displayer executes Velocity, the existing generic analyzer already warns admins before editing Velocity code. Note that warnings before editing documents with dangerous properties have only been introduced in XWiki 15.9, before that version, this was a known issue and the advice was simply to be careful. This vulnerability has been patched in XWiki 15.10.16, 16.4.7, and 16.10.2 by adding a required rights analyzer that warns the admin before editing about the possibly malicious code.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is that the system allows content created by a low-privileged user to be processed and executed with the elevated privileges of a high-privileged user (the administrator) who later interacts with that content. The low-privileged user cannot execute the script directly, but by storing it in a document, they can have it executed when an administrator saves that document. This is a failure in managing the privileges associated with the data, as the system incorrectly applies the administrator's rights to the content created by the untrusted user. The fix, which involves adding a "required rights analyzer," confirms that the weakness lies in the improper handling of privileges related to the content.

Answer: CWE-269

CVE Description:
Go-Guerrilla SMTP Daemon is a lightweight SMTP server written in Go. Prior to 1.6.7, when ProxyOn is enabled, the PROXY command will be accepted multiple times, with later invocations overriding earlier ones. The proxy protocol only supports one initial PROXY header; anything after that is considered part of the exchange between client and server, so the client is free to send further PROXY commands with whatever data it pleases. go-guerrilla will treat these as coming from the reverse proxy, allowing a client to spoof its IP address. This vulnerability is fixed in 1.6.7.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is that the Go-Guerrilla SMTP daemon is tricked into misusing its authority. The daemon is designed to trust a `PROXY` command coming from a reverse proxy to set the client's IP address. However, after the initial, legitimate `PROXY` command, it fails to transition to a state where subsequent `PROXY` commands are rejected. This allows a malicious client to send a second `PROXY` command, which the daemon incorrectly treats as another authoritative command from the proxy. The daemon is thus "confused" by the client into performing a privileged action (setting the source IP) on the client's behalf. This is a classic example of the Confused Deputy problem.

Answer: CWE-441

CVE Description:
LocalS3 is an Amazon S3 mock service for testing and local development. Prior to version 1.21, the LocalS3 service's bucket creation endpoint is vulnerable to XML External Entity (XXE) injection. When processing the CreateBucketConfiguration XML document during bucket creation, the service's XML parser is configured to resolve external entities. This allows an attacker to declare an external entity that references an internal URL, which the server will then attempt to fetch when parsing the XML. The vulnerability specifically occurs in the location constraint processing, where the XML parser resolves external entities without proper validation or restrictions. When the external entity is resolved, the server makes an HTTP request to the specified URL and includes the response content in the parsed XML document. This vulnerability can be exploited to perform server-side request forgery (SSRF) attacks, allowing an attacker to make requests to internal services or resources that should not be accessible from external networks. The server will include the responses from these internal requests in the resulting bucket configuration, effectively leaking sensitive information. The attacker only needs to be able to send HTTP requests to the LocalS3 service to exploit this vulnerability.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is the software's failure to properly restrict the processing of external entities within an XML document. The CVE description explicitly states that "the service's XML parser is configured to resolve external entities," which allows an attacker to craft an XML payload with a malicious external entity. This leads to the server making unintended requests, a classic XML External Entity (XXE) attack. CWE-611 specifically addresses this weakness. While the vulnerability leads to Server-Side Request Forgery (CWE-918), the SSRF is the *consequence* of the underlying XXE weakness, making CWE-611 the more precise root cause.

Answer: CWE-611

CVE Description:
Vyper is the Pythonic Programming Language for the Ethereum Virtual Machine. In versions up to and including 0.4.2rc1, `concat()` may skip evaluation of side effects when the length of an argument is zero. This is due to a fastpath in the implementation which skips evaluation of argument expressions when their length is zero. In practice, it would be very unusual in user code to construct zero-length bytestrings using an expression with side-effects, since zero-length bytestrings are typically constructed with the empty literal `b""`; the only way to construct an empty bytestring which has side effects would be with the ternary operator introduced in v0.3.8, e.g. `b"" if self.do_some_side_effect() else b""`. The fix is available in pull request 4644 and expected to be part of the 0.4.2 release. As a workaround, don't have side effects in expressions which construct zero-length bytestrings.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is a faulty optimization in the Vyper compiler. Specifically, for the `concat()` function, the compiler implements a "fastpath" that checks the length of an argument. If the length is determined to be zero, it skips the full evaluation of the expression that produces the argument. This is a flaw in the order of operations. The compiler should fully evaluate the expression first—including any side effects—and then use the result. By checking a property of the result (its length) before the expression is fully evaluated, it incorrectly skips the evaluation and its associated side effects. This directly corresponds to an incorrect order of operations at the compiler level.

CWE-480 (Use of Incorrect Operator Precedence or Order of Operations) best describes this flaw. While often associated with arithmetic operators, the principle applies to any situation where the sequence of evaluation steps is incorrect and violates the language's semantics, leading to unexpected behavior like skipping side effects.

Answer: CWE-480

CVE Description:

The attack may be initiated remotely. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in any way.
response:
The CVE description states that a "manipulation leads to session expiration" and that the attack can be "initiated remotely." This describes a scenario where an attacker can force a logged-in user's session to be terminated without their consent.

The most common and direct cause for this type of vulnerability is Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF). In a CSRF attack, an attacker tricks a victim's browser into sending an unintended, authenticated request to a web application. If the application's logout function (or any other session-terminating function) is not protected against CSRF, an attacker can craft a malicious link or web page that, when visited by the victim, will cause their browser to send a request to log them out, thus causing "session expiration." This perfectly matches the description of a remote "manipulation" leading to the specified outcome.

While other weaknesses like Incorrect Authorization (CWE-863) could also be a cause, CSRF (CWE-352) is a more specific and common weakness that directly maps to the described scenario of an external action causing a state change (session termination) within a user's authenticated session.

Answer: CWE-352

Further exploitation (e.g., bypassing file permission checking or remote kernel code execution) is potentially possible, though this has not been demonstrated. This scenario is a classic example of CWE-121, which specifically addresses buffer overflows that occur on the stack. The potential consequences mentioned in the CVE, such as causing a panic (Denial of Service) and the potential for remote code execution, are the direct and well-known impacts of this type of weakness.

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: iio: adc: ti-ads1119: fix information leak in triggered buffer The 'scan' local struct is used to push data to user space from a triggered buffer, but it has a hole between the sample (unsigned int) and the timestamp. This hole is never initialized. Initialize the struct to zero before using it to avoid pushing uninitialized information to userspace.
response:
The CVE description details an information leak vulnerability where a local structure (`scan`) is copied to userspace. The problem arises because there is padding ("a hole") within the structure that is never explicitly initialized. When the structure is copied, the uninitialized padding, which contains stale data from the kernel stack, is leaked to userspace.

The root cause is the failure to initialize a variable before it is used. The "use" in this case is the operation of copying the structure's contents to userspace, which exposes the uninitialized data. This scenario is a classic example of using a variable that contains indeterminate values.

CWE-457: Use of Uninitialized Variable is the most appropriate CWE as it directly describes this weakness. The code uses the `scan` variable (by copying it) without ensuring all of its memory, including compiler-added padding, has been properly initialized.

Answer: CWE-457

CVE Description:
HAXiam is a packaging wrapper for HAXcms which allows anyone to spawn their own microsite management platform. In versions 11.0.4 and below, the application returns a 200 response when requesting the data of a valid user and a 404 response when requesting the data of an invalid user. This can be used to infer the existence of valid user accounts. An authenticated attacker can use automated tooling to brute force potential usernames and use the application's response to identify valid accounts. This can be used in conjunction with other vulnerabilities, such as the lack of authorization checks, to enumerate and deface another user's sites. This is fixed in version 11.0.5.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is that the application's response differs based on whether the requested data (a user account) exists. A `200 OK` response for a valid user and a `404 Not Found` for an invalid user creates an observable discrepancy. An attacker can exploit this difference to determine which usernames are valid. This directly aligns with the definition of an observable response discrepancy, where the system's behavior leaks information.

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: Bluetooth: L2CAP: Fix memory leak in vhci_write Syzkaller reports a memory leak as follows: ==================================== BUG: memory leak unreferenced object 0xffff88810d81ac00 (size 240): [...] hex dump (first 32 bytes): 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 ................ 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 ................ backtrace: [<ffffffff838733d9>] __alloc_skb+0x1f9/0x270 net/core/skbuff.c:418 [<ffffffff833f742f>] alloc_skb include/linux/skbuff.h:1257 [inline] [<ffffffff833f742f>] bt_skb_alloc include/net/bluetooth/bluetooth.h:469 [inline] [<ffffffff833f742f>] vhci_get_user drivers/bluetooth/hci_vhci.c:391 [inline] [<ffffffff833f742f>] vhci_write+0x5f/0x230 drivers/bluetooth/hci_vhci.c:511 [<ffffffff815e398d>] call_write_iter include/linux/fs.h:2192 [inline] [<ffffffff815e398d>] new_sync_write fs/read_write.c:491 [inline] [<ffffffff815e398d>] vfs_write+0x42d/0x540 fs/read_write.c:578 [<ffffffff815e3cdd>] ksys_write+0x9d/0x160 fs/read_write.c:631 [<ffffffff845e0645>] do_syscall_x64 arch/x86/entry/common.c:50 [inline] [<ffffffff845e0645>] do_syscall_64+0x35/0xb0 arch/x86/entry/common.c:80 [<ffffffff84600087>] entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x63/0xcd ==================================== HCI core will uses hci_rx_work() to process frame, which is queued to the hdev->rx_q tail in hci_recv_frame() by HCI driver. Yet the problem is that, HCI core may not free the skb after handling ACL data packets. To be more specific, when start fragment does not contain the L2CAP length, HCI core just copies skb into conn->rx_skb and finishes frame process in l2cap_recv_acldata(), without freeing the skb, which triggers the above memory leak. This patch solves it by releasing the relative skb, after processing the above case in l2cap_recv_acldata().
response:
The vulnerability described is a classic memory leak. A resource, specifically a socket buffer (`skb`), is allocated for processing a data packet. However, in a particular conditional path—when a start fragment doesn't contain the L2CAP length—the code proceeds without freeing this `skb` after its purpose has been served. The memory is not released after its effective lifetime has ended, leading to a leak. This directly corresponds to the definition of a missing release of memory.

Answer: CWE-401

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: tty: xilinx_uartps: split sysrq handling lockdep detects the following circular locking dependency: CPU 0 CPU 1 ========================== ============================ cdns_uart_isr() printk() uart_port_lock(port) console_lock() cdns_uart_console_write() if (!port->sysrq) uart_port_lock(port) uart_handle_break() port->sysrq = ... uart_handle_sysrq_char() printk() console_lock() The fixed commit attempts to avoid this situation by only taking the port lock in cdns_uart_console_write if port->sysrq unset. However, if (as shown above) cdns_uart_console_write runs before port->sysrq is set, then it will try to take the port lock anyway. This may result in a deadlock. Fix this by splitting sysrq handling into two parts. We use the prepare helper under the port lock and defer handling until we release the lock.
response:
The CVE description explicitly details a "circular locking dependency" between two different locks (`uart_port_lock` and `console_lock`). This situation, where one process holds lock A and waits for lock B, while a second process holds lock B and waits for lock A, is the classic cause of a deadlock. The description itself uses the term "deadlock" to describe the potential outcome. Therefore, the most appropriate CWE is one that specifically addresses this condition.

Answer: CWE-833

CVE Description:
Craft is a content management system. Versions of Craft CMS on the 4.x branch prior to 4.14.13 and on the 5.x branch prior to 5.6.16 contains a potential remote code execution vulnerability via Twig SSTI. One must have administrator access and `ALLOW_ADMIN_CHANGES` must be enabled for this to work. Users should update to the patched versions 4.14.13 or 5.6.15 to mitigate the issue.
response:
The vulnerability is explicitly described as a "Twig SSTI," which stands for Server-Side Template Injection. This type of flaw occurs when an application improperly handles user-supplied input within a server-side template, allowing an attacker to inject template syntax that is then executed by the template engine on the server. This can lead to remote code execution, as stated in the description. The most precise CWE for this specific weakness is CWE-1336, which directly addresses the failure to neutralize special elements within a template engine context.

Answer: CWE-1336

CVE Description:
IBM Cloud Pak for Business Automation 18.0.0, 18.0.1, 18.0.2, 19.0.1, 19.0.2, 19.0.3, 20.0.1, 20.0.2, 20.0.3, 21.0.1, 21.0.2, 21.0.3, 22.0.1, and 22.0.2 allows restricting access to organizational data to valid contexts. response:
The vulnerability described is a failure in the access control mechanism. This is a classic case of a missing security check for a specific function.

CVE Description:
A reflected XSS vulnerability in RSform!Pro component 3.0.0 - 3.3.13 for Joomla was discovered. The issue arises from the improper handling of the filter[dateFrom] GET parameter, which is reflected unescaped in the administrative backend interface. This allows an authenticated attacker with admin or editor privileges to inject arbitrary JavaScript code by crafting a malicious URL.
response:
The vulnerability described is a classic case of reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS). The root cause is the application's failure to properly sanitize or encode user-supplied input from the `filter[dateFrom]` GET parameter before embedding it into the HTML output of the administrative interface. This "improper handling" and reflection of "unescaped" data allows an attacker to inject malicious scripts that are then executed by the victim's browser.

CWE-79, "Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')", is the most appropriate CWE as it directly addresses this fundamental flaw of not neutralizing user-controllable input before it is used to generate a web page.

Answer: CWE-79

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: scsi: lpfc: Fix null pointer dereference after failing to issue FLOGI and PLOGI If lpfc_issue_els_flogi() fails and returns non-zero status, the node reference count is decremented to trigger the release of the nodelist structure. However, if there is a prior registration or dev-loss-evt work pending, the node may be released prematurely. When dev-loss-evt completes, the released node is referenced causing a use-after-free null pointer dereference. Similarly, when processing non-zero ELS PLOGI completion status in lpfc_cmpl_els_plogi(), the ndlp flags are checked for a transport registration before triggering node removal. If dev-loss-evt work is pending, the node may be released prematurely and a subsequent call to lpfc_dev_loss_tmo_handler() results in a use after free ndlp dereference. Add test for pending dev-loss before decrementing the node reference count for FLOGI, PLOGI, PRLI, and ADISC handling.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is a Use-After-Free condition. The CVE description details a scenario where a resource, specifically a `nodelist` structure, is deallocated (its reference count is decremented, leading to its release) due to a failure in an operation like FLOGI or PLOGI. However, a separate, pending operation (`dev-loss-evt` work) still holds a reference to this structure. When this pending work eventually executes, it attempts to access the memory that has already been freed, leading to a "use-after-free" bug, which can manifest as a null pointer dereference. The core weakness is using a pointer to memory after it has been deallocated.

Answer: CWE-416

An attacker exploits this discrepancy to bypass the intended access control. This failure to treat different path representations as equivalent for security purposes is the core weakness.

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: bfq: Update cgroup information before merging bio When the process is migrated to a different cgroup (or in case of writeback just starts submitting bios associated with a different cgroup) bfq_merge_bio() can operate with stale cgroup information in bic. Thus the bio can be merged to a request from a different cgroup or it can result in merging of bfqqs for different cgroups or bfqqs of already dead cgroups and causing possible use-after-free issues. Fix the problem by updating cgroup information in bfq_merge_bio().
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is a race condition. A process's state (its cgroup membership) can change concurrently with I/O operations being processed for that process. The `bfq_merge_bio()` function uses cached cgroup information (`bic`) which can become stale if the process is migrated to a new cgroup. The function then operates on this outdated information, leading to incorrect merging between different cgroups or, in the worst case, using a reference to a cgroup that has been destroyed, resulting in a use-after-free. This is a classic Time-of-Check, Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) problem where the state is checked (or assumed) at one point and used later, without ensuring it hasn't changed in the interim.

Answer: CWE-362

CVE Description:
Unprotected Windows messaging channel ('Shatter') issue exists in Defense Platform Home Edition Ver.3.9.51.x and earlier. If an attacker sends a specially crafted message to the specific process of the Windows system where the product is running, arbitrary files in the system may be altered. As a result, an arbitrary DLL may be executed with SYSTEM privilege.
response:
The vulnerability described is a classic example of a "Confused Deputy" attack. A high-privilege process (the "deputy") is tricked by a low-privilege attacker into misusing its authority. The attacker sends a specially crafted message through an unprotected Windows messaging channel. The high-privilege process receives this message and, without properly validating the origin or the authority of the request, performs a privileged action (altering an arbitrary file) on the attacker's behalf. This pattern, where a privileged program is manipulated by a less-privileged entity to perform actions it shouldn't, is the essence of the Confused Deputy problem.

Answer: CWE-441

The CVE description and the included KASAN (Kernel Address Sanitizer) log explicitly identify the vulnerability as a "use-after-free". The log trace confirms this by showing the code path where the memory was allocated, the separate code path where it was freed (in `bfq_put_queue`), and the subsequent illegal read access to that same freed memory address within the `bfq_dispatch_request` function. This sequence of events—accessing a pointer after the memory it references has been deallocated—is the precise definition of a Use After Free weakness.

CVE Description:
IBM FlashSystem (IBM Storage Virtualize (8.5.0.0 through 8.5.0.13, 8.5.1.0, 8.5.2.0 through 8.5.2.3, 8.5.3.0 through 8.5.3.1, 8.5.4.0, 8.6.0.0 through 8.6.0.5, 8.6.1.0, 8.6.2.0 through 8.6.2.1, 8.6.3.0, 8.7.0.0 through 8.7.0.2, 8.7.1.0, 8.7.2.0 through 8.7.2.1) could allow a remote attacker with access to the system to execute arbitrary Java code due to improper restrictions in the RPCAdapter service.
response:
The CVE description states that a remote attacker can execute arbitrary Java code due to "improper restrictions in the RPCAdapter service". RPC (Remote Procedure Call) services in Java frequently rely on object serialization to pass data between the client and server. A common and severe vulnerability in such systems arises when the server deserializes data from an untrusted source without validating or restricting the types of objects being created. An attacker can craft a malicious serialized object payload which, upon being deserialized by the server, triggers a chain of method calls (a "gadget chain") that results in arbitrary code execution. This scenario is precisely described by CWE-502. The phrase "improper restrictions" directly points to the lack of controls over what classes are permitted during the deserialization process, which is the core of this weakness.

Answer: CWE-502

CVE Description:
parisneo/lollms-webui version V13 (feather) suffers from a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in the `POST /api/proxy` REST API. Attackers can exploit this vulnerability to abuse the victim server's credentials to access unauthorized web resources by specifying the JSON parameter `{"url":"http://steal.target"}`. Existing security mechanisms such as `forbid_remote_access(lollmsElfServer)`, `lollmsElfServer.config.headless_server_mode`, and `check_access(lollmsElfServer, request.client_id)` do not protect against this vulnerability.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is that the server-side application takes a user-supplied URL from the `/api/proxy` endpoint and makes a request to it without proper validation. This allows an attacker to force the server to send requests to arbitrary destinations, including internal network resources or external third-party services. This behavior is the exact definition of a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) weakness.

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: PCI/bwctrl: Fix NULL pointer dereference on bus number exhaustion When BIOS neglects to assign bus numbers to PCI bridges, the kernel attempts to correct that during PCI device enumeration. If it runs out of bus numbers, no pci_bus is allocated and the "subordinate" pointer in the bridge's pci_dev remains NULL. The PCIe bandwidth controller erroneously does not check for a NULL subordinate pointer and dereferences it on probe. Bandwidth control of unusable devices below the bridge is of questionable utility, so simply error out instead. This mirrors what PCIe hotplug does since commit 62e4492c3063 ("PCI: Prevent NULL dereference during pciehp probe"). The PCI core emits a message with KERN_INFO severity if it has run out of bus numbers. PCIe hotplug emits an additional message with KERN_ERR severity to inform the user that hotplug functionality is disabled at the bridge. A similar message for bandwidth control does not seem merited, given that its only purpose so far is to expose an up-to-date link speed in sysfs and throttle the link speed on certain laptops with limited Thermal Design Power. So error out silently. User-visible messages: pci 0000:16:02.0: bridge configuration invalid ([bus 00-00]), reconfiguring [...] pci_bus 0000:45: busn_res: [bus 45-74] end is updated to 74 pci 0000:16:02.0: devices behind bridge are unusable because [bus 45-74] cannot be assigned for them [...] pcieport 0000:16:02.0: pciehp: Hotplug bridge without secondary bus, ignoring [...] BUG: kernel NULL pointer dereference RIP: pcie_update_link_speed pcie_bwnotif_enable pcie_bwnotif_probe pcie_port_probe_service really_probe
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is the failure of the PCIe bandwidth controller code to verify that a pointer is not NULL before attempting to use it. The CVE description explicitly states that under certain conditions (bus number exhaustion), the "subordinate" pointer in the bridge's `pci_dev` remains NULL. The vulnerable code then "erroneously does not check for a NULL subordinate pointer and dereferences it on probe," leading to a kernel crash. This is a direct example of a NULL pointer dereference.

CVE Description:
A flaw was found in Keycloak. When an Active Directory user resets their password, the system updates it without performing an LDAP bind to validate the new credentials against AD. This vulnerability allows users whose AD accounts are expired or disabled to regain access in Keycloak, bypassing AD restrictions. The issue enables authentication bypass and could allow unauthorized access under certain conditions.
response:

The system successfully changes the password but fails to perform a subsequent authentication check (an LDAP bind) against the authoritative source, Active Directory. This missing step is essential to confirm the account's current status (i.e., not expired or disabled). By skipping this validation, the system incorrectly assumes the user is valid and grants access, leading to an authentication bypass. This directly corresponds to a missing critical step in an authentication mechanism.

CVE Description:
Erlang/OTP is a set of libraries for the Erlang programming language. In versions prior to OTP-27.3.4 (for OTP-27), OTP-26.2.5.12 (for OTP-26), and OTP-25.3.2.21 (for OTP-25), Erlang/OTP SSH fails to enforce strict KEX handshake hardening measures by allowing optional messages to be exchanged. This allows a Man-in-the-Middle attacker to inject these messages in a connection during the handshake. This issue has been patched in versions OTP-27.3.4 (for OTP-27), OTP-26.2.5.12 (for OTP-26), and OTP-25.3.2.21 (for OTP-25).
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is the failure of the Erlang/OTP SSH implementation to correctly adhere to the security requirements of the SSH protocol standard during the key exchange (KEX) handshake. The description explicitly states it "fails to enforce strict KEX handshake hardening measures," which is a deviation from a secure implementation of the standard. This leniency in protocol enforcement allows a Man-in-the-Middle attacker to inject messages that a correct implementation would reject. This directly maps to an improper implementation of a security check that is part of a standard.

Accessing such a variable can lead to unpredictable behavior, including crashes, as its value is indeterminate. In this case, the crafted HTTPS requests trigger a code path that uses an uninitialized variable, causing the AnyConnect VPN server process to restart, which results in a denial of service.

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: net: fix UaF in netns ops registration error path If net_assign_generic() fails, the current error path in ops_init() tries to clear the gen pointer slot. Anyway, in such error path, the gen pointer itself has not been modified yet, and the existing and accessed one is smaller than the accessed index, causing an out-of-bounds error: BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in ops_init+0x2de/0x320 Write of size 8 at addr ffff888109124978 by task modprobe/1018 CPU: 2 PID: 1018 Comm: modprobe Not tainted 6.2.0-rc2.mptcp_ae5ac65fbed5+ #1641 Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (Q35 + ICH9, 2009), BIOS 1.16.1-2.fc37 04/01/2014 Call Trace: <TASK> dump_stack_lvl+0x6a/0x9f print_address_description.constprop.0+0x86/0x2b5 print_report+0x11b/0x1fb kasan_report+0x87/0xc0 ops_init+0x2de/0x320 register_pernet_operations+0x2e4/0x750 register_pernet_subsys+0x24/0x40 tcf_register_action+0x9f/0x560 do_one_initcall+0xf9/0x570 do_init_module+0x190/0x650 load_module+0x1fa5/0x23c0 __do_sys_finit_module+0x10d/0x1b0 do_syscall_64+0x58/0x80 entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x72/0xdc RIP: 0033:0x7f42518f778d Code: 00 c3 66 2e 0f 1f 84 00 00 00 00 00 90 f3 0f 1e fa 48 89 f8 48 89 f7 48 89 d6 48 89 ca 4d 89 c2 4d 89 c8 4c 8b 4c 24 08 0f 05 <48> 3d 01 f0 ff ff 73 01 c3 48 8b 0d cb 56 2c 00 f7 d8 64 89 01 48 RSP: 002b:00007fff96869688 EFLAGS: 00000246 ORIG_RAX: 0000000000000139 RAX: ffffffffffffffda RBX: 00005568ef7f7c90 RCX: 00007f42518f778d RDX: 0000000000000000 RSI: 00005568ef41d796 RDI: 0000000000000003 RBP: 00005568ef41d796 R08: 0000000000000000 R09: 0000000000000000 R10: 0000000000000003 R11: 0000000000000246 R12: 0000000000000000 R13: 00005568ef7f7d30 R14: 0000000000040000 R15: 0000000000000000 </TASK> This change addresses the issue by skipping the gen pointer de-reference in the mentioned error-path. Found by code inspection and verified with explicit error injection on a kasan-enabled kernel.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is an incorrect handling of an error condition. When the `net_assign_generic()` function fails, the error-handling path in `ops_init()` proceeds to access a memory buffer (`gen`) using an index. However, because the allocation/resizing failed, the buffer is smaller than the error-handling code assumes it to be. This leads to an attempt to write to a memory location outside the boundaries of the allocated buffer. The KASAN report confirms this with a "slab-out-of-bounds" error on a "Write" operation. This is a classic example of an out-of-bounds write.

Answer: CWE-787

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: bpf, sockmap: Fix memleak in sk_psock_queue_msg If tcp_bpf_sendmsg is running during a tear down operation we may enqueue data on the ingress msg queue while tear down is trying to free it. sk1 (redirect sk2) sk2 ------------------- --------------- tcp_bpf_sendmsg() tcp_bpf_send_verdict() tcp_bpf_sendmsg_redir() bpf_tcp_ingress() sock_map_close() lock_sock() lock_sock() ... blocking sk_psock_stop sk_psock_clear_state(psock, SK_PSOCK_TX_ENABLED); release_sock(sk); lock_sock() sk_mem_charge() get_page() sk_psock_queue_msg() sk_psock_test_state(psock, SK_PSOCK_TX_ENABLED); drop_sk_msg() release_sock() While drop_sk_msg(), the msg has charged memory form sk by sk_mem_charge and has sg pages need to put. To fix we use sk_msg_free() and then kfee() msg. This issue can cause the following info: WARNING: CPU: 0 PID: 9202 at net/core/stream.c:205 sk_stream_kill_queues+0xc8/0xe0 Call Trace: <IRQ> inet_csk_destroy_sock+0x55/0x110 tcp_rcv_state_process+0xe5f/0xe90 ? sk_filter_trim_cap+0x10d/0x230 ? tcp_v4_do_rcv+0x161/0x250 tcp_v4_do_rcv+0x161/0x250 tcp_v4_rcv+0xc3a/0xce0 ip_protocol_deliver_rcu+0x3d/0x230 ip_local_deliver_finish+0x54/0x60 ip_local_deliver+0xfd/0x110 ? ip_protocol_deliver_rcu+0x230/0x230 ip_rcv+0xd6/0x100 ? ip_local_deliver+0x110/0x110 __netif_receive_skb_one_core+0x85/0xa0 process_backlog+0xa4/0x160 __napi_poll+0x29/0x1b0 net_rx_action+0x287/0x300 __do_softirq+0xff/0x2fc do_softirq+0x79/0x90 </IRQ> WARNING: CPU: 0 PID: 531 at net/ipv4/af_inet.c:154 inet_sock_destruct+0x175/0x1b0 Call Trace: <TASK> __sk_destruct+0x24/0x1f0 sk_psock_destroy+0x19b/0x1c0 process_one_work+0x1b3/0x3c0 ? process_one_work+0x3c0/0x3c0 worker_thread+0x30/0x350 ? process_one_work+0x3c0/0x3c0 kthread+0xe6/0x110 ? kthread_complete_and_exit+0x20/0x20 ret_from_fork+0x22/0x30 </TASK>
response:
The CVE description explicitly identifies the vulnerability as a "memleak". The root cause is a race condition where a message is allocated and enqueued (`sk_psock_queue_msg`) at the same time a teardown operation is trying to free the queue. In this specific path, the allocated message and its associated memory pages are not properly deallocated, leading to a memory leak. The fix involves adding the correct functions to free the message and its memory. This directly maps to the failure to release memory after its effective lifetime.

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: tcp: drop secpath at the same time as we currently drop dst Xiumei reported hitting the WARN in xfrm6_tunnel_net_exit while running tests that boil down to: - create a pair of netns - run a basic TCP test over ipcomp6 - delete the pair of netns The xfrm_state found on spi_byaddr was not deleted at the time we delete the netns, because we still have a reference on it. This lingering reference comes from a secpath (which holds a ref on the xfrm_state), which is still attached to an skb. This skb is not leaked, it ends up on sk_receive_queue and then gets defer-free'd by skb_attempt_defer_free. The problem happens when we defer freeing an skb (push it on one CPU's defer_list), and don't flush that list before the netns is deleted. In that case, we still have a reference on the xfrm_state that we don't expect at this point. We already drop the skb's dst in the TCP receive path when it's no longer needed, so let's also drop the secpath. At this point, tcp_filter has already called into the LSM hooks that may require the secpath, so it should not be needed anymore. However, in some of those places, the MPTCP extension has just been attached to the skb, so we cannot simply drop all extensions.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is a resource leak. Specifically, a `secpath` structure, which holds a reference to an `xfrm_state` object, is not released after it is no longer needed in the TCP receive path. This lingering reference prevents the `xfrm_state` from being deallocated. The problem becomes apparent when the associated network namespace is deleted, as the cleanup process detects the `xfrm_state` that should have already been freed. This failure to release a resource after its effective lifetime has ended is the core weakness.

Answer: CWE-401

CVE Description:
The strcmp implementation optimized for the Power10 processor in the GNU C Library version 2.39 and later writes to vector registers v20 to v31 without saving contents from the caller (those registers are defined as non-volatile registers by the powerpc64le ABI), resulting in overwriting of its contents and potentially altering control flow of the caller, or leaking the input strings to the function to other parts of the program.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is the `strcmp` function's failure to adhere to the Application Binary Interface (ABI) for the powerpc64le architecture. Specifically, the function modifies "non-volatile" (callee-saved) registers without saving their original values upon entry and restoring them before exiting. The caller function rightfully assumes these registers will be preserved across the function call. By failing to restore them, the `strcmp` function leaves the registers in an altered state, corrupting the caller's context. This failure to restore a resource (the registers) to its original state before returning control constitutes an incomplete cleanup.

Answer: CWE-459

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: tracing: kprobe: Fix memory leak in test_gen_kprobe/kretprobe_cmd() test_gen_kprobe_cmd() only free buf in fail path, hence buf will leak when there is no failure. Move kfree(buf) from fail path to common path to prevent the memleak. The same reason and solution in test_gen_kretprobe_cmd(). unreferenced object 0xffff888143b14000 (size 2048): comm "insmod", pid 52490, jiffies 4301890980 (age 40.553s) hex dump (first 32 bytes): 70 3a 6b 70 72 6f 62 65 73 2f 67 65 6e 5f 6b 70 p:kprobes/gen_kp 72 6f 62 65 5f 74 65 73 74 20 64 6f 5f 73 79 73 robe_test do_sys backtrace: [<000000006d7b836b>] kmalloc_trace+0x27/0xa0 [<0000000009528b5b>] 0xffffffffa059006f [<000000008408b580>] do_one_initcall+0x87/0x2a0 [<00000000c4980a7e>] do_init_module+0xdf/0x320 [<00000000d775aad0>] load_module+0x3006/0x3390 [<00000000e9a74b80>] __do_sys_finit_module+0x113/0x1b0 [<000000003726480d>] do_syscall_64+0x35/0x80 [<000000003441e93b>] entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x46/0xb0
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is a memory leak. A buffer is allocated, but the code to free this buffer (`kfree(buf)`) is only present in the error-handling or "fail path." If the function executes successfully, the free operation is skipped, and the allocated memory is never released. This leads to a gradual loss of available memory.

This scenario is a classic example of failing to release memory after its effective lifetime has ended, specifically because the release logic is missing from one or more code paths (in this case, the success path).

Answer: CWE-401

CVE Description:
Koa is expressive middleware for Node.js using ES2017 async functions. In koa < 2.16.1 and < 3.0.0-alpha.5, passing untrusted user input to ctx.redirect() even after sanitizing it, may execute javascript code on the user who use the app. This issue is patched in 2.16.1 and 3.0.0-alpha.5.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is the failure to properly sanitize or validate user-provided input before using it to generate a redirect URL. An attacker can supply a malicious URL using the `javascript:` scheme. When the application uses this input in the `ctx.redirect()` function, it generates an HTTP redirect response with a `Location` header pointing to the malicious `javascript:` URI. The victim's browser then executes the JavaScript code contained within the URI in the context of the vulnerable application's origin. This is a classic example of Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), where user input is improperly neutralized and reflected back to the user's browser, resulting in script execution.

Answer: CWE-79

A malicious actor can inject a specially crafted payload into the request, causing the browser to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the vulnerable page. This allows the malicious payload to be executed as script by the end-user's browser, which is the defining characteristic of Cross-Site Scripting as described by CWE-79.

CVE Description:
GPT-SoVITS-WebUI is a voice conversion and text-to-speech webUI. In versions 20250228v3 and prior, there is an unsafe deserialization vulnerability in bsroformer.py. The model_choose variable takes user input (e.g. a path to a model) and passes it to the uvr function. In uvr, a new instance of Roformer_Loader class is created with the model_path attribute containing the aformentioned user input (here called locally model_name). Note that in this step the .ckpt extension is added to the path. In the Roformer_Loader class, the user input, here called model_path, is used to load the model on that path with torch.load, which can lead to unsafe deserialization. At time of publication, no known patched versions are available.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is the use of the `torch.load` function on a file path that is derived from user-controlled input. The `torch.load` function uses Python's `pickle` utility for deserialization, which is inherently insecure when used on untrusted data. An attacker can craft a malicious model file (`.ckpt`) containing arbitrary code, and when the application attempts to load it via `torch.load`, the embedded code will be executed. This directly matches the weakness of deserializing data from an untrusted source.

Answer: CWE-502

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: btrfs: fix use-after-free on inode when scanning root during em shrinking At btrfs_scan_root() we are accessing the inode's root (and fs_info) in a call to btrfs_fs_closing() after we have scheduled the inode for a delayed iput, and that can result in a use-after-free on the inode in case the cleaner kthread does the iput before we dereference the inode in the call to btrfs_fs_closing(). Fix this by using the fs_info stored already in a local variable instead of doing inode->root->fs_info.
response:
The CVE description explicitly identifies the vulnerability as a "use-after-free on inode". This occurs because a pointer to an inode is used after the inode has been scheduled for deallocation (`iput`). A race condition allows the memory to be freed by a cleaner thread before it is accessed, leading to the use of a dangling pointer. This is the classic definition of a Use After Free weakness.

Answer: CWE-416

CVE Description:
Craft CMS stores arbitrary content provided by unauthenticated users in session files. This content could be accessed and executed, possibly using an independent vulnerability. Craft CMS redirects requests that require authentication to the login page and generates a session file on the server at '/var/lib/php/sessions'. Such session files are named 'sess_[session_value]', where '[session_value]' is provided to the client in a 'Set-Cookie' response header. Craft CMS stores the return URL requested by the client without sanitizing parameters. Consequently, an unauthenticated client can introduce arbitrary values, such as PHP code, to a known local file location on the server. Craft CMS versions 5.7.5 and 4.15.3 have been released to address this issue.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is the application's failure to sanitize user-provided input (the return URL) before writing it to a server-side session file. This allows an attacker to inject arbitrary content, specifically server-side PHP code, into a file on the server. The vulnerability is not just about improper input validation in general, but specifically about how that input is used to construct a file that contains executable code. This directly aligns with the definition of code injection, where externally-influenced input is used to generate code without proper neutralization of special elements.

Answer: CWE-94

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: cifs: Fix integer overflow while processing acregmax mount option User-provided mount parameter acregmax of type u32 is intended to have an upper limit, but before it is validated, the value is converted from seconds to jiffies which can lead to an integer overflow. Found by Linux Verification Center (linuxtesting.org) with SVACE.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is an integer overflow. The CVE description explicitly states, "cifs: Fix integer overflow while processing acregmax mount option." The vulnerability occurs because a user-supplied 32-bit unsigned integer is used in a multiplication operation (converting seconds to jiffies) *before* its value is validated against an upper limit. A sufficiently large input value will cause the result of the multiplication to exceed the maximum value that can be stored in the integer, causing it to wrap around. This is a classic example of an integer overflow vulnerability.

CVE Description:
File Browser provides a file managing interface within a specified directory and it can be used to upload, delete, preview, rename and edit files. In version 2.32.0 of the web application, all users have a scope assigned, and they only have access to the files within that scope. The Command Execution feature of Filebrowser allows the execution of shell commands which are not restricted to the scope, potentially giving an attacker read and write access to all files managed by the server. Until this issue is fixed, the maintainers recommend to completely disable `Execute commands` for all accounts. Since the command execution is an inherently dangerous feature that is not used by all deployments, it should be possible to completely disable it in the application's configuration. As a defense-in-depth measure, organizations not requiring command execution should operate the Filebrowser from a distroless container image. A patch version has been pushed to disable the feature for all existent installations, and making it opt-in. A warning has been added to the documentation and is printed on the console if the feature is enabled. Due to the project being in maintenance-only mode, the bug has not been fixed. Fix is tracked on pull request 5199.
response:

This is a classic case of a process or feature running with more permissions than are required for the task, allowing a lower-privileged user to perform actions at a higher privilege level. This directly corresponds to the weakness of executing with unnecessary privileges.

CVE Description:

Starting in version 1.2.0 and prior to version 1.2.2, users with access to Icinga Dependency Views, are allowed to see hosts and services that they weren't meant to on the dependency map. However, the name of an object will not be revealed nor does this grant access to a host's or service's detail view. Please note that this only affects the restrictions `filter/hosts` and `filter/services`. `filter/objects` is not affected by this and restricts objects as it is supposed to. Version 1.2.2 applies these restrictions properly. As a workaround, one may downgrade to version 1.1.3.
response:
The vulnerability described is a failure in the access control mechanism. Specifically, the Icinga DB Web application does not correctly apply configured access restrictions (`filter/hosts` and `filter/services`) within the dependency map view. This allows users to see the existence of hosts and services they are not authorized to view. The root cause is that the software fails to perform the necessary authorization check to filter out these objects based on the user's permissions in this specific context.

CWE-862: Missing Authorization is the most appropriate CWE. This weakness occurs when software does not perform an authorization check when it should. In this case, the code responsible for generating the dependency map is missing the step to check and apply the user's host and service filter restrictions, leading to an information leak.

Answer: CWE-862

CVE Description:
MyBB is free and open source forum software. Prior to version 1.8.39, the search component does not validate permissions correctly, which allows attackers to determine the existence of hidden (draft, unapproved, or soft-deleted) threads containing specified text in the title. The visibility state (`mybb_threads.visible` integer column) of threads is not validated in internal search queries, whose result is used to output a general success or failure of the search. While MyBB validates permissions when displaying the final search results, a search operation that internally produces at least one result outputs a redirect response (as a HTTP redirect, or a success message page with delayed redirect, depending on configuration). On the other hand, a search operation that internally produces no results outputs a corresponding message in the response without a redirect. This allows a user to determine whether threads matching title search parameters exist, including draft threads (`visible` with a value of `-2`), soft-deleted threads (`visible` with a value of `-1`), and unapproved threads (`visible` with a value of `0`); in addition to displaying generally visible threads (`visible` with a value of `1`). This vulnerability does not affect other layers of permissions. In order to exploit the vulnerability, the user must have access to the search functionality, and general access to forums containing the thread(s). The vulnerability does not expose the message content of posts. MyBB 1.8.39 resolves this issue.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is that the application's response changes based on the existence of data the user is not authorized to see. Specifically, if an internal search query finds a result (even a hidden one), the server issues a redirect. If it finds no results, it issues a standard message page. This difference in behavior is an observable side channel that leaks information—namely, the existence of a hidden thread matching a specific title. This is a classic example of an information leak via a behavioral discrepancy.

CWE-203: Observable Discrepancy is the most appropriate CWE because it specifically describes vulnerabilities where an attacker can infer secret information by observing differences in the system's responses or behavior. While a missing authorization check (like CWE-862) is the underlying reason for this behavior, the vulnerability's direct manifestation and exploit mechanism is the observable discrepancy itself.

Answer: CWE-203

CVE Description:
Deno is a JavaScript, TypeScript, and WebAssembly runtime. Starting in version 1.41.3 and prior to versions 2.1.13, 2.2.13, and 2.3.2, `deno run --allow-read --deny-read main.ts` results in allowed, even though 'deny' should be stronger. The result is the same with all global unary permissions given as `--allow-* --deny-*`. This only affects a nonsensical combination of flags, so there shouldn't be a real impact on the userbase. Users may upgrade to version 2.1.13, 2.2.13, or 2.3.2 to receive a patch.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is the incorrect logic used to resolve conflicting permission flags. The Deno runtime is presented with two contradictory instructions: `--allow-read` and `--deny-read`. The expected secure behavior is for the more restrictive `deny` policy to take precedence over the `allow` policy. However, the vulnerable versions incorrectly prioritize the `allow` flag, resulting in the process being granted read permissions when it should have been denied them. This is a classic case of a flawed authorization check where the logic for determining access rights is incorrect.

CWE-863: Incorrect Authorization is the most fitting classification. This CWE describes situations where the software performs an authorization check, but the logic of that check is flawed, leading to an incorrect decision about whether to grant or deny access to a resource or capability. In this case, the authorization logic incorrectly resolves the conflict between "allow" and "deny" rules.

CVE Description:
Label Studio is an open source data labeling tool. Prior to version 1.16.0, Label Studio's S3 storage integration feature contains a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in its endpoint configuration. When creating an S3 storage connection, the application allows users to specify a custom S3 endpoint URL via the s3_endpoint parameter. This endpoint URL is passed directly to the boto3 AWS SDK without proper validation or restrictions on the protocol or destination. The vulnerability allows an attacker to make the application send HTTP requests to arbitrary internal services by specifying them as the S3 endpoint. When the storage sync operation is triggered, the application attempts to make S3 API calls to the specified endpoint, effectively making HTTP requests to the target service and returning the response in error messages. This SSRF vulnerability enables attackers to bypass network segmentation and access internal services that should not be accessible from the external network. The vulnerability is particularly severe because error messages from failed requests contain the full response body, allowing data exfiltration from internal services. Version 1.16.0 contains a patch for the issue.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is that the application accepts a user-provided URL for the S3 endpoint and uses it to make a server-side HTTP request without properly validating that the URL points to a legitimate and intended destination. This allows an attacker to coerce the server into sending requests to arbitrary internal network services. This pattern is the exact definition of a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF).

Answer: CWE-918

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: USB: hub: Ignore non-compliant devices with too many configs or interfaces Robert Morris created a test program which can cause usb_hub_to_struct_hub() to dereference a NULL or inappropriate pointer: Oops: general protection fault, probably for non-canonical address 0xcccccccccccccccc: 0000 [#1] SMP DEBUG_PAGEALLOC PTI CPU: 7 UID: 0 PID: 117 Comm: kworker/7:1 Not tainted 6.13.0-rc3-00017-gf44d154d6e3d #14 Hardware name: FreeBSD BHYVE/BHYVE, BIOS 14.0 10/17/2021 Workqueue: usb_hub_wq hub_event RIP: 0010:usb_hub_adjust_deviceremovable+0x78/0x110 ... Call Trace: <TASK> ? die_addr+0x31/0x80 ? exc_general_protection+0x1b4/0x3c0 ? asm_exc_general_protection+0x26/0x30 ? usb_hub_adjust_deviceremovable+0x78/0x110 hub_probe+0x7c7/0xab0 usb_probe_interface+0x14b/0x350 really_probe+0xd0/0x2d0 ? __pfx___device_attach_driver+0x10/0x10 __driver_probe_device+0x6e/0x110 driver_probe_device+0x1a/0x90 __device_attach_driver+0x7e/0xc0 bus_for_each_drv+0x7f/0xd0 __device_attach+0xaa/0x1a0 bus_probe_device+0x8b/0xa0 device_add+0x62e/0x810 usb_set_configuration+0x65d/0x990 usb_generic_driver_probe+0x4b/0x70 usb_probe_device+0x36/0xd0 The cause of this error is that the device has two interfaces, and the hub driver binds to interface 1 instead of interface 0, which is where usb_hub_to_struct_hub() looks. We can prevent the problem from occurring by refusing to accept hub devices that violate the USB spec by having more than one configuration or interface.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is the kernel's failure to validate that the USB device's configuration adheres to the expected specification for a hub. The code implicitly trusts that a hub device will have only one interface and proceeds based on this assumption. When a non-compliant device with multiple interfaces is connected, this assumption is violated, leading the driver to access memory incorrectly and causing a NULL pointer dereference. The fix is to explicitly check for and reject devices that do not conform to the specification. This failure to check data provided by an external source against a set of rules is a classic case of improper input validation.

Answer: CWE-20

response:

CVE Description:
An issue in Kanaries Inc Pygwalker before v.0.4.9.9 allows a remote attacker to obtain sensitive information and execute arbitrary code via the redirect_path parameter of the login redirection function.
response:
The CVE description specifies that the vulnerability exists in a "login redirection function" and is triggered via the "redirect_path parameter". This indicates that the application takes a user-supplied path or URL and redirects the user's browser to it without proper validation. An attacker can manipulate this parameter to redirect a user to a malicious site for phishing (obtaining sensitive information) or potentially to a `javascript:` URI to execute client-side code. This specific type of weakness, where an application redirects to a user-controlled, unvalidated URL, is defined as an Open Redirect.

Answer: CWE-601

CVE Description:
The WP Front-end login and register plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to Reflected Cross-Site Scripting via the email and wpmp_reset_password_token parameters in all versions up to, and including, 2.1.0 due to insufficient input sanitization and output escaping. This makes it possible for unauthenticated attackers to inject arbitrary web scripts in pages that execute if they can successfully trick a user into performing an action such as clicking on a link.
response:
The vulnerability described is a classic case of Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS). The root cause is the application's failure to properly sanitize or escape user-supplied data from the `email` and `wpmp_reset_password_token` parameters before embedding it into the HTML output. This allows an attacker to craft a URL containing malicious scripts, which are then "reflected" off the web server to the victim's browser and executed in the context of the trusted site.

CWE-79 is the specific enumeration for this weakness, as it covers the failure to neutralize user-controllable input before it is included in a dynamically generated web page.

Answer: CWE-79

This sequence of freeing a memory location and then subsequently dereferencing the dangling pointer to that location is the definition of a Use After Free vulnerability.

CVE Description:
The ProfileGrid – User Profiles, Groups and Communities plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to PHP Object Injection in all versions up to, and including, 5.9.4.5 via deserialization of untrusted input in the get_user_meta_fields_html function. This makes it possible for authenticated attackers, with Subscriber-level access and above, to inject a PHP Object. No known POP chain is present in the vulnerable software, which means this vulnerability has no impact unless another plugin or theme containing a POP chain is installed on the site. If a POP chain is present via an additional plugin or theme installed on the target system, it may allow the attacker to perform actions like delete arbitrary files, retrieve sensitive data, or execute code depending on the POP chain present.
response:
The most appropriate CWE is CWE-502: Deserialization of Untrusted Data.

Justification: The CVE description explicitly states the vulnerability is "PHP Object Injection" which occurs "via deserialization of untrusted input". This is the exact definition of CWE-502. The application takes serialized data provided by an authenticated but potentially malicious user and processes it without verifying its integrity, allowing the attacker to instantiate arbitrary objects in memory. The discussion of POP chains is a common element of exploiting this type of vulnerability, further confirming that deserialization is the root cause.

Answer: CWE-502

response:
The vulnerability allows an attacker with physical access to a locked device to view sensitive user information. This is a classic case of information exposure.

CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor accurately represents this root cause. The "unauthorized actor" is the individual with physical access but without the credentials to unlock the device, and the weakness is that the system reveals sensitive information to them.

CVE Description:
If Brocade Fabric OS before Fabric OS 9.2.0 configuration settings are not set to encrypt SNMP passwords, then the SNMP privsecret / authsecret fields can be exposed in plaintext. The plaintext passwords can be exposed in a configupload capture or a supportsave capture if encryption of passwords is not enabled. An attacker can use these passwords to fetch values of the supported OIDs via SNMPv3 queries. There are also a limited number of MIB objects that can be modified.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is that sensitive information, specifically SNMP credentials (`privsecret` / `authsecret`), is stored in plaintext within configuration and support files if an encryption setting is not enabled. An attacker with access to these files can directly read the credentials. This is a classic example of storing sensitive data in an unencrypted, human-readable format.

CWE-312: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information directly addresses this weakness. The product stores sensitive data (the SNMP secrets) in a cleartext format, which can be read by an attacker who obtains the configuration or support files.

Answer: CWE-312

CVE Description:
A vulnerability was found in Ruby. The Ruby interpreter is vulnerable to the Marvin Attack. This attack allows the attacker to decrypt previously encrypted messages or forge signatures by exchanging a large number of messages with the vulnerable service.
response:
The root cause of the Marvin Attack is a timing side-channel vulnerability. The attack works by sending specially crafted messages to a service performing RSA decryption and measuring the precise time it takes for the service to respond with an error. Differences in response time, which depend on how the invalid padding is processed, leak small amounts of information about the private key. By repeating this process many times, an attacker can reconstruct the private key. This mechanism of leaking information through variations in execution time is the definition of an observable timing discrepancy.

Answer: CWE-208

CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: hrtimers: Handle CPU state correctly on hotplug Consider a scenario where a CPU transitions from CPUHP_ONLINE to halfway through a CPU hotunplug down to CPUHP_HRTIMERS_PREPARE, and then back to CPUHP_ONLINE: Since hrtimers_prepare_cpu() does not run, cpu_base.hres_active remains set to 1 throughout. However, during a CPU unplug operation, the tick and the clockevents are shut down at CPUHP_AP_TICK_DYING. On return to the online state, for instance CFS incorrectly assumes that the hrtick is already active, and the chance of the clockevent device to transition to oneshot mode is also lost forever for the CPU, unless it goes back to a lower state than CPUHP_HRTIMERS_PREPARE once. This round-trip reveals another issue; cpu_base.online is not set to 1 after the transition, which appears as a WARN_ON_ONCE in enqueue_hrtimer(). Aside of that, the bulk of the per CPU state is not reset either, which means there are dangling pointers in the worst case. Address this by adding a corresponding startup() callback, which resets the stale per CPU state and sets the online flag. [ tglx: Make the new callback unconditionally available, remove the online modification in the prepare() callback and clear the remaining state in the starting callback instead of the prepare callback ]
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is the failure to properly reset or re-initialize the per-CPU state for high-resolution timers when a CPU aborts a hot-unplug operation and comes back online. The system leaves stale data (e.g., `hres_active` remains set, `online` is not reset, pointers are dangling) because the necessary initialization logic for this specific state transition is missing. This leads to an inconsistent state where other parts of the kernel make incorrect assumptions about the timer's status. This failure to correctly initialize a resource upon entering a new state is a classic case of improper initialization.

Answer: CWE-665
