CVE Description:
In the Linux kernel, the following vulnerability has been resolved: virtio-blk: don't keep queue frozen during system suspend Commit 4ce6e2db00de ("virtio-blk: Ensure no requests in virtqueues before deleting vqs.") replaces queue quiesce with queue freeze in virtio-blk's PM callbacks. And the motivation is to drain inflight IOs before suspending. block layer's queue freeze looks very handy, but it is also easy to cause deadlock, such as, any attempt to call into bio_queue_enter() may run into deadlock if the queue is frozen in current context. There are all kinds of ->suspend() called in suspend context, so keeping queue frozen in the whole suspend context isn't one good idea. And Marek reported lockdep warning[1] caused by virtio-blk's freeze queue in virtblk_freeze(). [1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-block/ca16370e-d646-4eee-b9cc-87277c89c43c@samsung.com/ Given the motivation is to drain in-flight IOs, it can be done by calling freeze & unfreeze, meantime restore to previous behavior by keeping queue quiesced during suspend.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is a deadlock condition. The CVE description explicitly states that freezing the block queue for the entire duration of a system suspend "is also easy to cause deadlock." This occurs because the frozen queue (a locked resource) prevents other necessary kernel operations from proceeding, as they may need to access that same queue. The reference to a "lockdep warning" further confirms that the issue is a locking problem leading to a circular wait or a halt in execution, which is the definition of a deadlock.

CVE Description:
A vulnerability was found in systemd-coredump. This flaw allows an attacker to force a SUID process to crash and replace it with a non-SUID binary to access the original's privileged process coredump, allowing the attacker to read sensitive data, such as /etc/shadow content, loaded by the original process. A SUID binary or process has a special type of permission, which allows the process to run with the file owner's permissions, regardless of the user executing the binary. This allows the process to access more restricted data than unprivileged users or processes would be able to. An attacker can leverage this flaw by forcing a SUID process to crash and force the Linux kernel to recycle the process PID before systemd-coredump can analyze the /proc/pid/auxv file. If the attacker wins the race condition, they gain access to the original's SUID process coredump file. They can read sensitive content loaded into memory by the original binary, affecting data confidentiality.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is a race condition, specifically a Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) flaw. The "check" is the system identifying that a specific Process ID (PID) has crashed. The "use" is `systemd-coredump` later reading the metadata for that PID from the `/proc` filesystem to determine how to handle the coredump file (e.g., what permissions to set). An attacker can win a race to have the kernel reassign the now-vacant PID to a new, unprivileged process before `systemd-coredump` performs its "use" step. This causes `systemd-coredump` to use the metadata of the attacker's new process, leading it to apply incorrect, permissive settings to the coredump of the original privileged process.

An attacker with low-privileged access who can write DLL files to the monitored directory can achieve arbitrary code execution with SYSTEM privileges. Affected products include Panda Global Protection 2016, Panda Antivirus Pro 2016, Panda Small Business Protection, and Panda Internet Security 2016 (all versions up to 16.1.2).
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is that a high-privilege application (`PSEvents.exe`) loads a Dynamic-Link Library (DLL) from a location that is controllable by a low-privileged user. The application's search path for this resource includes an insecure, "user-writable directory." An attacker can place a malicious DLL in this directory, and the privileged application will load and execute it, leading to privilege escalation. This specific weakness is best described as an uncontrolled search path element.

Answer: CWE-427

CVE Description:
Invoice Ninja before 5.10.43 allows remote code execution from a pre-authenticated route when an attacker knows the APP_KEY. This is exacerbated by .env files, available from the product's repository, that have default APP_KEY values. The route/{hash} route defined in the invoiceninja/routes/client.php file can be accessed without authentication. The parameter {hash} is passed to the function decrypt that expects a Laravel ciphered value containing a serialized object. (Furthermore, Laravel contains several gadget chains usable to trigger remote command execution from arbitrary deserialization.) Therefore, an attacker in possession of the APP_KEY is able to fully control a string passed to an unserialize function.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is that the application takes user-controlled data (the `{hash}` parameter, which is an encrypted string), decrypts it, and then passes the result to a deserialization function (`unserialize`). Because the attacker can control the content of the serialized object by encrypting it with a known key, they can leverage gadget chains within the Laravel framework to achieve remote code execution. This is a direct instance of deserializing untrusted data.

CVE Description:
Gitk is a Tcl/Tk based Git history browser. Starting with 1.7.0, when a user clones an untrusted repository and runs gitk without additional command arguments, files for which the user has write permission can be created and truncated. The option Support per-file encoding must have been enabled before in Gitk's Preferences. This option is disabled by default. The same happens when Show origin of this line is used in the main window (regardless of whether Support per-file encoding is enabled or not). This vulnerability is fixed in 2.43.7, 2.44.4, 2.45.4, 2.46.4, 2.47.3, 2.48.2, 2.49.1, and 2.50.1.
response:
The root cause of this vulnerability is that `gitk` uses data from an untrusted Git repository to construct a command that is then executed by the operating system. Specifically, features like per-file encoding (defined in `.gitattributes`) or showing a line's origin can cause `gitk` to shell out to an external command. If the repository is crafted with malicious data containing special shell characters, these characters are not properly neutralized, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands on the user's system. This leads to the described impact of arbitrary file creation and truncation. This scenario is a classic example of OS Command Injection.

CVE Description:
Issue summary: A timing side-channel which could potentially allow recovering the private key exists in the ECDSA signature computation. Impact summary: A timing side-channel in ECDSA signature computations could allow recovering the private key by an attacker. However, measuring the timing would require either local access to the signing application or a very fast network connection with low latency. There is a timing signal of around 300 nanoseconds when the top word of the inverted ECDSA nonce value is zero. This can happen with significant probability only for some of the supported elliptic curves. In particular the NIST P-521 curve is affected. To be able to measure this leak, the attacker process must either be located in the same physical computer or must have a very fast network connection with low latency. For that reason the severity of this vulnerability is Low. The FIPS modules in 3.4, 3.3, 3.2, 3.1 and 3.0 are affected by this issue.
response:
The vulnerability described is a classic timing side-channel attack. The CVE explicitly states that "a timing side-channel... exists in the ECDSA signature computation" and that an attacker can recover the private key by "measuring the timing" of the operation. The time taken for the computation varies depending on an internal, secret value (the nonce), which creates an observable discrepancy. This directly maps to the definition of an observable timing discrepancy weakness.

Answer: CWE-208
